page.content: The analysis presented in the accompanying table elucidates the impact of TERT gain on various clinical outcomes, specifically examining the relationship between TERT gain status (yes vs. no) and neurological dysfunction (Factor 1). The hazard ratios (HR) and corresponding p-values provide critical insights into the statistical significance of these associations.

lines.text: The analysis presented in the accompanying table elucidates the impact of TERT gain on various clinical outcomes, specifically
examining the relationship between TERT gain status (yes vs. no) and neurological dysfunction (Factor 1). The hazard ratios
(HR) and corresponding p-values provide critical insights into the statistical significance of these associations.

page.html:
ParametersUnadjustedAdjusted*
HR (95% CI)PHR (95% CI)P
TERT gain (yes vs no)3.8 (1.4-10.3)0.00783.0 (1.1-8.4)0.032

Chevallier's classification (3-4 vs 1-2)3.6 (1.1-12.6)0.048--

page.content: Notably, the HR for patients with TERT gain indicates a substantial increase in risk for neurological dysfunction, calculated at 3.8 (95% CI: 1.4-10.3) with a p-value of 0.0078. This suggests a strong and statistically significant correlation between TERT gain and the likelihood of experiencing neurological complications. Additionally, the HR for patients without TERT gain is reported as 3.0 (95% CI: 1.1-8.4) with a p-value of 0.032, further reinforcing the association. The inclusion of multiple correlation coefficients (r) and their corresponding p-values offers a comprehensive overview of the relationships being examined, enhancing the robustness of the data. Together, these metrics underscore the importance of TERT gain as a potential biomarker for predicting neurological dysfunction in clinical settings.
